title: Lamb colostrum management
subtitle: The first six hours are the ones that matter most.
excerpt: A newborn lamb that does not receive adequate colostrum in the first six hours of life is vulnerable to infection, hypothermia, and neonatal diseases that colostrum antibodies would have prevented. Colostrum management is the single highest-impact intervention in a lambing kit. Most lamb deaths are traceable to inadequate colostrum intake.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef & Lamb colostrum management factsheet, ahdb.org.uk. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Sheep (2023). SAC Consulting Scotland, Sheep Disease Focus: colostrum and neonatal health.

glossaryTerms.definition: The first milk produced by the ewe after lambing, rich in maternal antibodies (immunoglobulins), energy, and growth factors. A lamb's gut can absorb these antibodies directly into the bloodstream only in the first 12 to 24 hours of life. The absorption window closes completely by 24 hours.

content.text: in the first six hours gives the lamb its immune system. A lamb without it is open to infection from the start. Everything else in the lambing kit matters less if the lamb goes without colostrum in those first few hours.

A lamb needs at least 200 ml of colostrum per kg of bodyweight in the first 24 hours, around 500 ml for a 2.5 kg lamb. Of this, 50 ml per kg should be consumed within the first two hours, before the gut starts to close to antibody absorption. A ewe with a full udder and a lamb that feeds actively from birth usually achieves this without assistance. Problems arise with multiple births, large lambs, weak lambs, and ewes with low colostrum production.

Check that the ewe's teats are clear of the wax plug that blocks the teat canal just before lambing. Express a stream of colostrum from both teats to confirm milk is available before leaving the ewe with a new lamb. Return an hour later to check the lamb has a full belly, a well-fed lamb's abdomen is rounded; a hungry lamb's flanks are tucked in. A lamb that is not feeding by 30 to 40 minutes after birth needs assistance.

A stomach tube (a soft rubber catheter and syringe) allows colostrum to be delivered directly into the lamb's stomach when suckling is not possible. Warm the colostrum to body temperature (39°C) before tubing. Pass the tube gently through the mouth to the stomach, checking that the tube is in the oesophagus not the airway by feeling for its outline on the left side of the neck. Deliver the colostrum slowly and remove the tube without pinching or kinking it. Tube equipment is available from most agricultural vets and suppliers.

Freeze ewe colostrum from productive ewes at the start of lambing for use in emergencies. Label bags by date and volume. Thaw in warm water at 45°C, above 50°C and antibody proteins denature. Powdered colostrum substitute is a backup, not an equivalent; commercial ewe's milk replacer contains antibody concentrates but at a lower titre than fresh or frozen ewe colostrum.

and spray the navel with 7% iodine within the first hour of birth to seal the navel cord against joint-ill bacteria. A dry, correctly treated navel shrinks to a cord within two to three days. A wet, swollen, or malodorous navel at 24 hours needs a vet assessment, joint-ill (polyarthritis) following umbilical infection is a serious welfare problem in lambs.
